title: Protein Ice Cream
tagline: Creami-style pint with protein math that actually checks out.
description: Protein ice cream the way people actually make it in 2026: a Ninja Creami pint that spins up scoopable and creamy, with a blender-and-freezer fallback if you don't own the machine. Two scoops of powder per pint, so 25g protein per serving is arithmetic, not marketing.
ingredients: 1 1/2 cups (360ml) milk of choice (2% or whole = creamier)
2 scoops (60-65g) whey or whey-casein blend protein powder (about 50g protein total)
1 Tbsp sugar-free instant vanilla pudding mix (the texture secret)
1 Tbsp maple syrup or honey (or 2 tsp allulose)
Optional mix-ins: chocolate chips, crushed cookies, peanut butter
steps: Whisk milk, protein powder, pudding mix, sweetener, and salt until completely smooth (or blend 10 seconds - no longer, or you'll whip in foam).
Pour into a Creami pint. Do not pass the max fill line. Freeze on a flat surface for 24 hours.
Spin on the Lite Ice Cream setting. It will come out crumbly and chalky-looking. That's normal.
Add 1 Tbsp milk and hit Re-spin. Now it's ice cream.
For mix-ins: dig a hole to the bottom, drop them in, run the Mix-in cycle.

No machine? Pour the base into an ice cube tray, freeze solid, then blitz the cubes in a food processor with a splash of milk until it looks like soft-serve. Eat it immediately - it doesn't re-freeze well.
notes: The math: one 30-32g scoop of whey has 24-25g protein. Two scoops per pint = about 50g, so half a pint = 25g. The old one-scoop-split-two-ways version was a 13g serving pretending to be 25.
Pudding mix isn't optional if you want creamy - its modified starch blocks the ice crystals that make protein bases icy.
Whey-casein blends spin creamier than pure whey isolate. Plant proteins work but come out denser.
testedNote: Old version claimed 25g protein per serving from one scoop split across two servings (about 13g); rebuilt as a two-scoop Creami pint with a freezer-only fallback.

verdict.note: With a Creami and pudding mix it's legitimately creamy; the no-machine fallback is decent soft-serve you have to eat on the spot.

troubleshooting: Crumbly and chalky after the first spin → normal for high-protein bases; add 1 Tbsp milk and re-spin.
Icy instead of creamy → you skipped the pudding mix or used watery milk; the starch is what blocks ice crystals.
Frozen pint has a hollow, foamy top → over-blended base; whisk gently and let any foam settle before freezing.
Freezer-fallback batch sets like a brick → that's what protein and water do; re-blitz with a splash of milk instead of trying to scoop it.
